mappings.rationaleAI: Scientific study of mind and behavior, directly encompassing attention (A), sensation and perception (B, C), and the various schools (behaviorist, cognitive) that shaped theories of attention and perception.
Anatomy and function of the outer, middle, and inner ear, covering sound transduction via cochlear hair cells (B.3.a, B.3.b) and semicircular canals for vestibular balance (B.3.d, B.6).

Comprehensive coverage of sensory modalities including mechanoreceptors, photoreceptors, chemoreceptors, thermoreceptors (B.1), proprioception (B.5), vestibular sense (B.6), and additional modalities beyond the classical five senses (B.4, B.7, B.8).

Awareness of internal and external events (A), subjective experience and its neural correlates relevant to perceptual processing (C), and altered states of consciousness bordering on hallucinatory phenomena (G.2).

Visual perception arising from cone cell activation in the retina (B.2.a), transduction of light into neural signals (B.2.b), and perceptual processes including trichromacy, color mixing, and metamerism (B.2.c).

The sensory organ for photoreception, covering retinal photoreceptor cells (B.2.a), transduction of light to neural signals (B.2.b), and visual acuity and color perception via cone and rod photoreceptors (B.2.c).
Mechanical wave phenomenon covering frequency, amplitude, and speed as physical properties (B.3.a) and psychoacoustic perception of pitch, loudness, timbre, and spatial location (B.3.b).

Central organ of nervous system responsible for sensory integration, including physiological mechanisms of attention (A.5), cerebral cortex responses to visual stimuli (B.2.d), and auditory pathway processing in the central nervous system (B.3.b).

Clinically significant disturbances in cognition and perception, including neurobiological factors in hallucinations (G.2.a) and types of hallucinatory experience arising from psychotic and neurodevelopmental disorders (G.2.b).
Psychoanalytic theory of unconscious influences on attention (A.4) and individual differences in perceptual functioning shaped by personality, drives, and unconscious processes (C.4).
Filmmaking technique creating the illusion of motion through sequential display of images, directly illustrating visual cues for perceiving movement (F.1).

Information theory (Shannon) quantifies information in bits inversely proportional to event probability, directly applicable to the analysis of attentive phenomena in terms of information theory (A.6).
Full range of human responses to internal and external stimuli, encompassing attention to the environment (A) and cognitive behavior including perception and decision-making (C).
